title: Measuring and marking for joinery
subtitle: How to set out joints accurately using gauges, squares, and the marking knife
excerpt: Joinery accuracy depends entirely on marking out: a joint sawn to an inaccurate line cannot be corrected by fitting. This reading covers the datum face, marking gauges, the combination square, the marking knife, and the working sequence for setting out a joint from scratch.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the joinery marking-out sequence; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the marking gauge and knife technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: A tool with a beam, a sliding fence, and a steel pin or blade. Used to scribe a line parallel to a face or edge at a set distance.
A multi-function layout tool combining a rule, a 90-degree square head, a 45-degree mitre head, and a spirit level.
A knife with a single bevel, used to score lines across the grain. The knife cut gives a cleaner registration than a pencil.
The reference face from which all measurements are taken. Every piece needs a datum face and a datum edge before marking begins.

content.text: The difference between a joint that fits cleanly and one that requires force comes down to marking out. A saw or chisel cannot correct a line that is in the wrong place. The tools used in joinery marking are simple: a
. The working order is fixed: establish the datum face first, then use that face for all subsequent gauging.
The datum face and datum edge
Before any marking begins, select and mark the

: the flattest, most accurate face of the piece. Mark it with a pencil loop: an open curl symbol on the face pointing to the datum edge. The datum edge is the straightest edge, perpendicular to the datum face. All gauging in a joint marking sequence must come from these two references. Mixing reference faces produces a joint that is offset even when both pieces are individually accurate.

fence to the required distance from the datum face. Lock the fence with the thumbscrew. Test the setting on a piece of scrap before marking the real piece. Hold the gauge with the fence tight to the face and drag the pin toward you in one continuous stroke. Two strokes over the same line with the gauge rocking produces a double line; a single steady stroke produces a clean single score. The pin should be just sharp enough to scribe; a very sharp pin catches the grain and wanders off-line.

checks for squareness and marks shoulder lines across the face and edges. To mark a square shoulder, hold the square head against the datum edge and draw a pencil or knife line along the blade. Check the square itself by marking a line, flipping the square, and checking whether the line registers exactly on the flipped blade. A square that has been dropped may be out of true and should be checked regularly.

to mark shoulder lines across the grain. The single bevel cuts cleanly on one side, leaving a vertical wall the saw can register against. Score against the combination square or try square blade, keeping the flat face of the knife tight against the guide. Deepen the line with a second pass, then use the chisel to pare a small V-groove at the line so the tenon saw sits in the groove rather than wandering off it.
Mark datum face and datum edge on all pieces first. Do not proceed without this step.
Set the marking gauge to one dimension and mark all pieces that require that setting before moving on. Resetting the gauge introduces error.
Score shoulder lines with the marking knife, not a pencil. A knife line is 0.2 mm wide; a pencil line is 0.5 to 1 mm and introduces that amount of ambiguity into every cut.

Check each piece against the plan before cutting. Mark the waste side of every line with a small pencil X; this prevents sawing on the wrong side, which removes an extra saw-kerf width from the joint.
